frozen_components: Sparse Autoencoder (pre-trained, used for feature extraction only)
Reference model activations (frozen for retention matching)
SAE decoder matrix (read-only for subspace construction)
architecture.summary: SSPU is a three-stage unlearning framework that leverages Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to guide targeted weight updates in LLMs. Stage 1 performs data-driven layer and feature selection by computing mean-squared activations on forget and retain sets, identifying SAE features most and least associated with the forget topic. Stage 2 constructs two orthonormal subspaces via QR decomposition: a 'forget-relevant' subspace (from top-k decoder vectors) and a 'forget-irrelevant' subspace (from bottom-k decoder vectors). Stage 3 applies constrained optimization that projects forget-batch activations into the irrelevant subspace while restricting weight updates to the relevant subspace, preserving retain-set activations via a retention loss term.
trained_components: Loss weighting parameters (α, β, γ coefficients)

biblio.title: Model Unlearning via Sparse Autoencoder Subspace Guided Projections

claims.statement: SSPU reduces harmful knowledge accuracy by 3.22% compared to the strongest baseline (RMU)
SSPU achieves 23.91% accuracy on WMDP-Cyber forget set
SSPU raises average utility score (MMLU, TruthfulQA, GSM8K) by 2.88% over RMU

claims.dataset: Four jailbreak datasets (obfuscation, roleplay, instruction override, narrative)
claims.statement: SSPU reduces malicious accuracy under jailbreak prompts by 13.59% versus SAE-based unlearning

claims.dataset: Four jailbreak datasets (obfuscation, roleplay, instruction override, narrative)
claims.statement: SSPU reduces malicious accuracy under jailbreak prompts by 2.83% versus RMU
Layer 3 yields the greatest accuracy reduction (strongest unlearning effect) compared to deeper layers in the 26-layer gemma-2b-it model
SSPU relies on availability of a well-trained sparse autoencoder; applicability may be constrained in settings where a suitable SAE is unavailable or difficult to train
The approach does not explicitly guarantee that unrelated capabilities outside the relevant subspace remain entirely unaffected
